gaius_marius_convo_0_n01.npcDialogue: I beat the Cimbri when every other general Rome sent came home in a box. Seven consulships. And now they want to give my command to a man who was drinking wine in Athens while I was burying legions in the mud of Gaul. So tell me — why should I listen to you?

options.playerDialogue: Because you need allies who haven't already chosen sides. I'm still free to choose mine.
Because Sulla is organizing faster than you are, and someone needs to tell you that.
Because I served under your standard at Vercellae. I know what you're capable of.

gaius_marius_convo_0_n02.npcDialogue: Free to choose. That's a luxury most men in this city have already spent. The optimates have their lists, the populares have their martyrs, the Italians have their grievances. A man who hasn't committed is either wise or useless. Which are you?

options.playerDialogue: Let me prove which one I am.

gaius_marius_convo_0_n03.npcDialogue: I know exactly how fast Sulla is moving. I've been watching him since he served under me in Africa — an adequate officer with an inadequate bloodline and unlimited ambition. He learned everything he knows from me, and he thinks that makes him my equal. It doesn't.

options.playerDialogue: Then what's your plan to stop him?

gaius_marius_convo_0_n04.npcDialogue: Vercellae. That was the last day Rome was Rome. Every man in that line knew he might die, and not one of them broke. I remember the men who stood. Sit. We have things to discuss.

gaius_marius_convo_0_n05.npcDialogue: The Mithridatic command is everything. Whoever leads Rome's legions against the eastern king controls the treasury of Asia Minor. That's not just military glory — it's the power to reshape the Republic. Sulla thinks it's his. The senate will try to give it to him. What do you think should happen?

responses.playerDialogue: The senate assigned the command by law. Overturning that sets a precedent worse than Sulla himself.
I think you and Sulla should share the command. A divided war is better than a civil one.
Take it. You earned it at Vercellae. Let Sulla argue with your legions.
Use the tribunes. Sulpicius can transfer the command through legislative action. Perfectly legal, technically.

fallbackResponse.playerDialogue: I think Rome should decide, not one man's ambition.

gaius_marius_convo_0_n06.npcDialogue: You speak like a man who hasn't watched his life's work stolen by a patrician with better dinner invitations. Let me ask you something more direct.

gaius_marius_convo_0_n07.npcDialogue: I like your instincts. Sulpicius is already drafting the legislation. When the vote comes, it will be chaos — the optimates will try to obstruct with religious declarations, the tribunes will push through anyway. I need someone in the forum who can hold a crowd. Not with speeches. With presence.

options.playerDialogue: I'll be in the forum. Point me at the crowd you need held.
Crowds are unpredictable. I'd rather work the senators' clients directly.

gaius_marius_convo_0_n08.npcDialogue: The populares are my natural base, but they're not enough. I need the Italian veterans — men who remember serving under me. They're citizens now, or close enough. If I could guarantee their vote in the assemblies, Sulla's senate backing means nothing. Can you reach those men?

options.playerDialogue: I have contacts among the Italian veterans in the city. I'll sound them out.
The Italian veterans are a weapon that cuts both ways. I won't be the one who lights that fire.

gaius_marius_convo_0_n09.npcDialogue: I see. You keep your distance. That's your right. But understand this — in the months ahead, distance will not protect you. Everyone in Rome will be forced to declare, one way or another.

gaius_marius_convo_0_n10.npcDialogue: Good. You've shown me something today. I don't trust easily — the men I've trusted have a way of turning up on proscription lists. But you're useful, and you're willing. That's enough for now. When I move against Sulla, you'll know before the senate does.

gaius_marius_convo_0_n11.npcDialogue: You tried. That counts for something, even when the result falls short. Stay close. I have a long memory for men who make the effort, and a longer one for men who don't.

gaius_marius_convo_0_n12.npcDialogue: Then go. Keep your clean hands and your careful neutrality. When Sulla marches his legions through the forum, we'll see how much good it does you.

deltas.reason: Marius considers you a reliable operative
Sulla's network marks you as a Marian agent
Committed to the old general's dangerous gambit
Marius notes your willingness, if not your success
Marius has no time for fence-sitters
Kept your options open at the cost of a powerful man's respect
